statement.markdown: Given an array of non-negative integers and a target sum, implement a dynamic programming algorithm to calculate the maximum score that can be achieved by selecting a subset of elements such that the sum of the selected elements is equal to the target sum. The maximum score is the sum of the scores of the selected elements.
statement.inputFormat: The first line of input contains an integer n, which is the number of elements in the array. The following line contains n space-separated integers representing the array elements. The next line contains an integer target, which is the target sum.
statement.outputFormat: Print the maximum score that can be achieved.
statement.notes: The algorithm should work in O(n*target) time complexity and O(n*target) space complexity.

examples.explanation: Select 10, 20, and 30 to get a maximum score of 60.

hints.text: First hint text: The maximum score can be calculated by considering each element in the array and deciding whether to include it in the selected subset or not.

editorial.title: Dynamic Programming Algorithm for Maximum Score Subsequence
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved using a dynamic programming approach by creating a 2D table where each cell [i][j] represents the maximum score that can be achieved by considering the i-th element and a sum of j.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve trying all possible subsets of elements and calculating their sum. This approach would result in a time complexity of O(2^n), which is inefficient for large arrays.
editorial.betterSolution: The dynamic programming approach has a time complexity of O(n*target) and a space complexity of O(n*target).
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves filling up the dp table in a bottom-up manner, where each cell [i][j] is filled based on the maximum score that can be achieved from the previous cells.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the algorithm can be proven by considering the base cases and the recurrence relation.
editorial.dryRun: To understand the algorithm, consider a dry run with a small input array and a target sum.
commonMistakes: Not considering all possible subsets of elements in the brute force approach
Not using a 2D table to store intermediate results in the dynamic programming approach

functionSignature.cpp: int maxScore(int* nums, int n, int target);
functionSignature.java: int maxScore(int[] nums, int n, int target);
functionSignature.python: def max_score(self, nums: list[int], n: int, target: int) -> int;
